There were more dollars spent on construction projects in the amusement and recreation (up <hide>.<hide> percent), religious (up <hide>.<hide> percent), communications (up <hide>.<hide> percent), office (up <hide>.<hide> percent) and educational (up <hide>.<hide> percent) segments, among others.
In addition to manufacturing, the other major sectors with decreased construction spending in March included health care (down <hide>.<hide> percent), commercial (down <hide>.<hide> percent) and lodging (down <hide>.
Meanwhile, private residential construction spending fell <hide>.<hide> percent in March, but with <hide>.<hide> percent growth over the past <hide> months.
In the latest data, single-family and multifamily construction declined <hide>.<hide> percent and <hide>.<hide> percent in March, respectively.
More encouragingly, single-family activity jumped <hide>.

In addition to those components, public construction spending was flat in March, with a gain of <hide>.<hide> percent over the past year.
The Dallas Federal Reserve Bank reported that manufacturing activity continued to expand solidly in April.
The composite index of general business conditions declined from <hide>.<hide> in April, but growth remained strong overall.
In fact, most of the key measures of activity accelerated in the latest survey, including new orders (up from <hide>.<hide> to <hide>.<hide>), production (up from <hide>.<hide>), capacity utilization (up from <hide>.<hide>), shipments (up from <hide>.<hide>), employment (up from <hide>.<hide>), hours worked (up from <hide>.<hide>) and capital expenditures (up from <hide>.<hide>).

Looking specifically at durable goods activity in March, new orders strengthened for primary metals (up <hide>.<hide> percent), computers and electronic products (up <hide>.<hide> percent), electrical equipment, appliances and components (up <hide>.<hide> percent) and fabricated metal products (up <hide>.
In contrast, sales decreased for machinery (down <hide>.<hide> percent), furniture and related products (down <hide>.<hide> percent) and motor vehicles and parts (down <hide>.
Meanwhile, shipments of manufactured goods rose <hide>.<hide> percent in March, with durable and nondurable goods shipments increasing <hide>.<hide> percent, respectively.
On a year-over-year basis, factory shipments have risen <hide>.
Core capital goods shipments pulled back <hide>.<hide> percent from February's all-time high, but with year-over-year growth of <hide>.

The No.
<hide> seed Shelton High boys volleyball team swept visiting the Fairfield Warde/Ludlowe Coop, <hide>-<hide> (<hide>-<hide>, <hide>-<hide>, <hide>-<hide>) in the first round of the Class L state tournament on Tuesday afternoon at Murray Gymnasium.
Seeded No.
<hide>, Fairfield (<hide>-<hide>) rallied from a <hide>-<hide> deficit to get within a point at <hide>-<hide>.
After the teams traded serves, Grant Fevrier finished off the sweep for Shelton with a searing kill through Fairfield's defense.
Shelton advances to face third-seeded Southington High on Friday, June <hide>, at <hide> p.m.
